C001.claim: Think of action as a measure of "effort" over time—systems choose paths that balance energy trade-offs, not just the shortest or fastest route.
C001.query: Least action principle Think of action as a measure of "effort" over time—systems choose paths that balance energy trade-offs, physics lecture notes textbook explanation
C001.result_snippet: Darwin & Evolution: Organisms that survive tend to use minimal energy for maximum return (e.g., cheetahs conserving energy between sprints). Physics: The Principle of Least Action, developed by Maupertuis and Euler (1700s), suggests that systems follow paths that minimize the total action—an early version of the least effort idea. Classical physics is grounded in the Principle of Least Action, yet no comparably general variational framework exists for far-from-equilibrium, dissipative, and path- dependent systems. We introduce the Minimum Effort Principle, formalized through the Minimum Effort Transition Path (METP), which characterizes the empirically realized trajectories connecting dynamically coherent states in ... The Least Effort Principle posits that organisms, whether biological entities or complex systems, will naturally select the path of action that requires the minimum expenditure of resources--primarily energy, time, and attention--to achieve a desired outcome. Tonight, in my Radical Hope community of practice, we explored the Law of Least Effort — showing up across Taoism (Wu Wei), physics (Least Action), and psychology (Zipf's Principle). Find out why the brain chooses the least effort and how that forms habits. A journey into the brain's energy saving, psychoanalysis, and evolution.
C001.source_policy: Prefer stable accepted physics sources; avoid novelty unless topic requires it.
